children.name: Hypothymis
children.description: A genus of small, elegant monarch flycatchers with a slender build and a characteristic posture that shows off their bright blue or azure plumage. Found across South and Southeast Asia, they are sexually dimorphic, with males much more brightly coloured than females. They hawk for insects in the forest understorey and are often members of mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Hypothymis azurea
children.description: A small, graceful monarch with the male a brilliant azure blue with a black nape band and a black bill, found across South and Southeast Asia. It is an active, acrobatic insectivore that gleans and sallies for prey in the forest understorey. Its clear, whistled call carries through the forests where it resides.

children.name: Hypothymis helenae
children.description: A striking blue monarch with a short, erectile crest and a black patch on the nape, endemic to the Philippine islands. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging for insects in the mid-storey and canopy. Its call is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Hypothymis coelestis
children.description: A brilliant cerulean-blue monarch with a white belly and a distinct black stripe through the eye, found in the Philippines on the islands of Luzon, Mindanao, and Samar. It inhabits the canopy of lowland rainforests and feeds on insects taken in flight. Its striking plumage makes it one of the most sought-after monarchs in its range.

children.name: Arascania longipennis
children.description: A small, strikingly colored bird with a melodious song. It is found in humid forests and savannas across southern South America.

children.name: Arctanthus leucophaea
children.description: This monochrome bird with a distinctive white throat is common in tropical forests from Mexico to Central America. It has a clear, flute-like song.

children.name: Clytorhynchus halorus
children.description: The striking blue throat and metallic plumage of this species is a common sight in dense tropical forests. It has a piercing, whistling call.

children.name: Dasylopa albertae
children.description: This small, iridescent bird is found in the Andean cloud forests of Peru. It has a unique and melodious song that it uses to attract mates.

children.name: Eumegamimus ocellatus
children.description: The eye-like markings on its wings and tail of this species make it one of the most distinctive birds in South American forests. It is a vocal bird with a complex song.

children.name: Helianthides cinerascens
children.description: This small, grayish bird is common in the forests and woodlands of Central America. Its song is a series of clear notes that it repeats.

children.name: Helianthides leucophaeus
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its melodious song has a distinctive quality to it.

children.name: Helianthides phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song is a high-pitched whistle that it repeats.

children.name: Heliodictee leucotis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of Central America. Its song is a series of clear notes that it repeats.

children.name: Incaenigma albiventris
children.description: This small, white-winged bird is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Incaenigma leucophaeus
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Incaenigma phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Irisitta leucophaea
children.description: A small, white-throated bird that is common in the forests of Central America. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Irisitta phillippinensis
children.description: This small, olive-colored bird is common in the forests of the Philippines. Its song has a distinct quality to it.

children.name: Terpsiphone
children.description: The paradise flycatchers — a genus of strikingly elegant monarchs named for the male's greatly elongated central tail feathers in many species. They are found across Africa, Asia, and the Indian Ocean islands. Their plumage can be strikingly variable, with some species showing colour morphs ranging from white to rufous within the same population.

children.name: Terpsiphone atrocaudata
children.description: A graceful monarch with elongated central tail feathers, the male a glossy black with a white belly and a blue eye-ring, breeding in Japan, Korea, and Taiwan and wintering in Southeast Asia. It catches insects in mid-air with elegant sallies from exposed perches in the forest canopy. Its simple, whistled song is a characteristic sound of its temperate forest breeding grounds.

children.name: Terpsiphone viridis
children.description: A stunning flycatcher with the male showing two colour morphs — rufous or white — both with a glossy blue-black head and long flowing tail streamers, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa. It is an active, vocal bird of woodlands and gardens, often seen in noisy family parties after breeding. Its harsh, rasping call is an unexpected contrast to its elegant appearance.

children.name: Terpsiphone paradisi
children.description: The Indian Paradise Flycatcher has the male with a glossy black head, elongated central tail streamers, and either rufous or white body plumage. It is widespread across the Indian subcontinent, often found in well-wooded gardens and forests. Its harsh, rasping call and elegant flight displays make it a familiar garden bird.

children.name: Terpsiphone affinis
children.description: Blyth's Paradise Flycatcher has a glossy blue-black head, chestnut or white body plumage, and long tail streamers. It ranges from the eastern Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. It inhabits broadleaf evergreen forests and forages for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Terpsiphone incei
children.description: The Amur Paradise Flycatcher is a long-tailed monarch with the male typically in white morph with a glossy black head. It breeds in eastern China, Korea, and the Russian Far East and migrates to Southeast Asia. It inhabits forests and parks, sallying for insects from exposed perches.

children.name: Terpsiphone cinnamomea
children.description: A small paradise flycatcher with entirely rufous-chestnut plumage, a bluish-grey eye-ring, and short tail streamers, endemic to the Philippine islands. It inhabits lowland and montane rainforests, foraging actively in the mid-storey. Its call is a series of harsh, rasping notes.

children.name: Terpsiphone cyanescens
children.description: A striking paradise flycatcher with the male a deep blue above and pale below, with long tail streamers, found in the Philippines on the islands of Palawan and the Calamianes. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the canopy. Its song is a clear, whistled melody.

children.name: Terpsiphone atrochalybeia
children.description: A stunning paradise flycatcher endemic to Sao Tome Island in the Gulf of Guinea, with the male a deep metallic blue-black with long tail streamers. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Terpsiphone bedfordi
children.description: Bedford's Paradise Flycatcher is a striking bird with a glossy blue-black head, white underparts, and long tail streamers, endemic to the montane forests of the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. It is found in the Kai Bala and Itombwe highlands at elevations above 1,500 metres. It is classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Terpsiphone rufiventer
children.description: A handsome paradise flycatcher with a glossy blue-black head, chestnut belly, and rufous upperparts, found across the forests of Central and West Africa. The male has moderately elongated tail streamers. It forages actively in the forest understorey and canopy.

children.name: Terpsiphone batesi
children.description: Bates's Paradise Flycatcher has a glossy blue-black head, grey upperparts, and white underparts, found in the lowland forests of Cameroon, Gabon, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. It is a small paradise flycatcher with relatively short tail streamers. It forages for insects in the mid-storey of rainforest.

children.name: Terpsiphone corvina
children.description: The Malagasy Paradise Flycatcher has the male a glossy black with long tail streamers and the female a rufous-brown with a grey head, endemic to the forests of eastern Madagascar. It inhabits humid rainforests and forages actively in the canopy. Its song is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Terpsiphone bourbonnensis
children.description: A small paradise flycatcher with a glossy blue-black head, grey upperparts, and white underparts, found on the islands of Reunion and Mauritius in the Indian Ocean. The male has long tail streamers while the female lacks them. It inhabits native forests and gardens.

children.name: Terpsiphone mutata
children.description: The Comoros Paradise Flycatcher has the male with a glossy blue-black head, rufous or white body plumage, and long tail streamers, found across the Comoros Islands and northern Madagascar. It inhabits forests, gardens, and plantations. Its distinctive call is a harsh, rasping note.

children.name: Terpsiphone rufocinerea
children.description: A small paradise flycatcher with a glossy blue-black head, grey upperparts, and a rufous belly and vent, found in the lowland forests of Central Africa. The male has short tail streamers. It inhabits swamp forests and riparian woodlands.

children.name: Terpsiphone smithii
children.description: The Annobon Paradise Flycatcher is endemic to the island of Annobon in Equatorial Guinea, with a glossy blue-black head, chestnut upperparts, and white underparts. It inhabits the island's forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its extremely restricted range.

children.name: Terpsiphone periophthalmica
children.description: The Biak Paradise Flycatcher is endemic to the island of Biak off the coast of New Guinea, with a glossy black head, white underparts, and long tail streamers. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Terpsiphone chrysorhina
children.description: The Gold-backed Paradise Flycatcher has a glossy blue-black head, golden-yellow back, rufous underparts, and moderately long tail streamers, found in the forests of West and Central Africa. It inhabits lowland rainforests and forages for insects in the canopy. Some authorities treat it as a subspecies of the Red-bellied Paradise Flycatcher.

children.name: Grallina
children.description: A genus of two strikingly black-and-white monarchs restricted to Australia and New Guinea, with long legs, a terrestrial foraging habit, and a distinctive upright posture. Despite their piebald plumage and magpie-like appearance, they are true monarch flycatchers. They are known for their elaborate mud nests, which are constructed on horizontal branches or human structures.

children.name: Grallina cyanoleuca
children.description: A familiar black-and-white bird with a thin white bill and a melodious, piping call, found across Australia and southern New Guinea. Despite its common name, it is not a magpie nor a lark but a monarch flycatcher that forages on the ground for insects. It builds a distinctive mud bowl nest lined with grass and feathers, often placed on a horizontal branch.

children.name: Grallina bruijnii
children.description: A striking black-and-white monarch found along fast-flowing streams and rivers in the montane forests of New Guinea. It is smaller than the Magpie-lark and lacks the white rump. It forages along boulder-strewn riverbanks and is often seen in pairs.

children.name: Monarcha
children.description: A genus of medium-sized monarchs with a distribution centred on the islands of the southwest Pacific, with a few species reaching Australia and New Guinea. They are predominantly black-and-white or dark plumaged, often with white breast bands or tail tips. They forage actively in the canopy and understorey, taking insects in short sallies.

children.name: Monarcha cinerascens
children.description: A medium-sized, grey-brown monarch with a white throat and breast, a dark eye-stripe, and white edges to the tail, found on islands from Sulawesi to the Solomons. It is an adaptable monarch that inhabits forests, mangroves, and secondary growth across its extensive island range. It forages actively in the canopy, sallying for insects.

children.name: Monarcha melanopsis
children.description: The Black-faced Monarch has a distinctive black face mask, blue-grey upperparts, and rich rufous-orange underparts. It breeds in rainforests of eastern Australia and migrates north to New Guinea in winter.

children.name: Monarcha frater
children.description: The Black-winged Monarch has blue-grey upperparts, black wings, and rich rufous underparts. It is found in lowland and hill forests of New Guinea and northern Australia.

children.name: Monarcha erythrostictus
children.description: The Red-bellied Monarch has a black head, blue-grey upperparts, and a rich chestnut-red belly contrasting with white underparts. It is endemic to the Solomon Islands, inhabiting lowland and hill forests. It forages actively in the canopy for insects.

children.name: Monarcha vidua
children.description: A striking black-and-white monarch with a black head, throat, and upperparts contrasting with a white belly and wing patches, found in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland forests and forest edges. Its call is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Monarcha boanensis
children.description: A critically endangered monarch endemic to the island of Boano in the Moluccas, with a black head, white throat, and chestnut underparts. It inhabits lowland forest on limestone, a habitat severely threatened by deforestation. It is one of the rarest monarchs in the world.

children.name: Monarcha everetti
children.description: The White-tipped Monarch has a black head, white throat, chestnut underparts, and white tips to the tail feathers, endemic to the island of Tanahjampea in the Lesser Sundas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Monarcha loricatus
children.description: The Black-tipped Monarch has a black head, blue-grey upperparts, chestnut underparts, and black tips to the tail feathers, found on the islands of Buru and Seram in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland and hill forests and forages for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Monarcha castaneiventris
children.description: The Chestnut-bellied Monarch has a black head, blue-grey upperparts, and a rich chestnut belly and underparts, found in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland and hill forests, often following mixed-species foraging flocks. Its song is a series of whistled notes.

children.name: Myiagra
children.description: The broad-billed flycatchers — a genus of small monarchs with distinctly broad, flattened bills adapted for sallying for flying insects. They are found from Southeast Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. They are restless foragers, constantly on the move and flicking their wings and tails, with many species showing glossy blue-black or metallic upperparts in the males.

children.name: Myiagra alecto
children.description: A striking monarch with the male a deep iridescent blue-black and the female a rich rufous-orange above with a white throat and breast, found in northern Australia and New Guinea. It inhabits dense vegetation along waterways, mangroves, and rainforest edges. It sallies from low perches to catch insects in flight.

children.name: Myiagra ruficollis
children.description: A small monarch with a noticeably broad, flat bill, slate-blue upperparts, and a rufous-orange throat and breast, found in coastal northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas. It is a specialist of mangroves and coastal forests, where it hunts insects in short, swift sallies from exposed perches. Its broad bill is an adaptation for catching flying insects in dense coastal vegetation.

children.name: Myiagra rubecula
children.description: The Leaden Flycatcher is a small, soberly coloured monarch with leaden-grey upperparts, whitish underparts, and a slight crest. It is a restless forager of forest canopies across northern and eastern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Myiagra cyanoleuca
children.description: The Satin Flycatcher has the male a glossy deep blue-black above and white below, while the female is grey-brown with a rufous throat. It breeds in wet forests of eastern Australia and winters in New Guinea.

children.name: Myiagra inquieta
children.description: The Restless Flycatcher is a distinctive bird with glossy blue-black upperparts, white underparts, and a prominent erectile crest. It is named for its constant wing-flicking and tail-pumping behaviour, and its whirring, grinding call resembles scissors being sharpened.

children.name: Myiagra nana
children.description: The Paperbark Flycatcher is a small, grey-brown monarch with a pale throat and breast, found in paperbark swamps and riparian woodlands of northern Australia. It is a quiet, unobtrusive forager that sallies for insects from low perches.

children.name: Myiagra ferrocyanea
children.description: A small monarch with the male a deep blue-grey above and white below, and the female with a rufous throat, endemic to the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth, sallying for insects. Its call is a high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Myiagra cervinicauda
children.description: The Makira Flycatcher is endemic to the island of Makira in the Solomon Islands, with olive-grey upperparts and pale underparts. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging actively in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Myiagra caledonica
children.description: A small monarch with blue-grey upperparts, white underparts, and a white throat, found across New Caledonia, Vanuatu, and the Loyalty Islands. It inhabits forests and forest edges, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its song is a rapid series of high-pitched notes.

children.name: Myiagra vanikorensis
children.description: A small monarch with dark grey upperparts, white underparts, and a pale grey throat, found on Vanikoro Island in the Solomon Islands and nearby Santa Cruz Islands. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the canopy.

children.name: Myiagra albiventris
children.description: The Samoan Flycatcher is endemic to the islands of Upolu and Savaii in Samoa, with glossy blue-black upperparts and a white belly. It inhabits forests and plantations, sallying for insects from exposed perches. It is classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Myiagra azureocapilla
children.description: A striking monarch with a blue cap, black mask, chestnut back, and white underparts, found in Fiji. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging actively for insects. Its bright plumage makes it one of the most distinctive Fijian flycatchers.

children.name: Myiagra castaneigularis
children.description: The Chestnut-throated Flycatcher has glossy blue-black upperparts, a rich chestnut throat, and white underparts, found in Fiji. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves, sallying for insects. It is often seen in pairs or small family groups.

children.name: Myiagra hebetior
children.description: The Dull Flycatcher has plain brown upperparts and pale underparts, found in the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands. Despite its name, it is a typical Myiagra in behaviour, sallying for insects from exposed perches. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth.

children.name: Myiagra pluto
children.description: The Biak Flycatcher is a small, dark monarch endemic to the island of Biak off the coast of New Guinea, with dark grey upperparts and a pale belly. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Myiagra galeata
children.description: The Dark Flycatcher has dark brownish-grey upperparts and pale underparts, found in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves, foraging for insects in the mid-storey. Its song is a series of thin, high-pitched notes.

children.name: Myiagra oceanica
children.description: The Oceanic Flycatcher has glossy blue-black upperparts and white underparts, found in the Caroline Islands of Micronesia. It inhabits forests and plantations, foraging for insects in the canopy. Its call is a sharp, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Myiagra erythrops
children.description: The Palau Flycatcher is endemic to the Palau Islands in Micronesia, with glossy blue-black upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive pinkish-red eye-ring. It inhabits native forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Myiagra atra
children.description: The Black Flycatcher is a small, entirely dark-plumaged monarch endemic to the Aru Islands off the coast of New Guinea. The male is glossy blue-black while the female is dark brown. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known. Its restricted range makes it vulnerable to habitat loss.

children.name: Myiagra eichhorni
children.description: Eichhorn's Flycatcher is a small monarch found in the Bismarck Archipelago, with dark grey upperparts and pale underparts. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the Dull Flycatcher. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects.

children.name: Myiagra melvillensis
children.description: The Melville Flycatcher is endemic to Melville Island and Bathurst Island off northern Australia, with leaden-grey upperparts and pale underparts. It is sometimes considered a subspecies of the Leaden Flycatcher. It inhabits coastal forests and mangroves.

children.name: Symposiachrus
children.description: A genus of monarchs with striking black-and-white or boldly patterned plumage, found across the islands of Southeast Asia and the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Symposiachrus trivirgatus
children.description: The Spectacled Monarch has a dark grey head with white eye-rings, white underparts, and chestnut-orange flanks. It inhabits lowland forests and mangroves from the Lesser Sundas to the Moluccas and New Guinea.

children.name: Symposiachrus guttula
children.description: The Spot-winged Monarch is a small, black-and-white monarch with white spots on the wings and tail, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and the surrounding islands.

children.name: Symposiachrus axillaris
children.description: The Fantailed Monarch is a small, dark monarch with a white patch on the wing and a broad, fan-shaped tail. It inhabits montane forests of New Guinea at mid to high elevations.

children.name: Symposiachrus rubiensis
children.description: A striking monarch with rich rufous underparts, a black head and throat, and a white belly, found in the lowland forests of New Guinea. It forages actively in the canopy, sallying for insects. Its call is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Symposiachrus julianae
children.description: The Kofiau Monarch is a critically endangered monarch endemic to the island of Kofiau in the Moluccas, with a black head, white throat, and rich chestnut underparts. It inhabits lowland forests on limestone, severely threatened by habitat loss. It is one of the rarest birds in Indonesia.

children.name: Symposiachrus brehmii
children.description: The Biak Monarch is a striking black-and-white monarch endemic to the island of Biak off the coast of New Guinea, with black upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive white-tipped tail. It inhabits lowland forests and is threatened by deforestation. It is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Symposiachrus sacerdotum
children.description: The Flores Monarch is a critically endangered monarch endemic to the island of Flores in the Lesser Sundas, with a black head, white throat, and chestnut underparts. It inhabits lowland forests on limestone and has been severely impacted by deforestation. Fewer than 250 mature individuals are estimated to remain.

children.name: Symposiachrus leucurus
children.description: The White-tailed Monarch has a black head and upperparts, white underparts, and a white-tipped tail, endemic to the islands of the Kai and Tanimbar groups in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy. Its call is a series of clear whistles.

children.name: Symposiachrus godeffroyi
children.description: The Yap Monarch is endemic to the island of Yap in Micronesia, with a black head, white throat, and yellow underparts. It inhabits forests and secondary growth, foraging for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range and habitat loss.

children.name: Symposiachrus menckei
children.description: The Mussau Monarch is endemic to Mussau Island in the Bismarck Archipelago, with a black head, white throat, and chestnut underparts. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Symposiachrus browni
children.description: Brown's Monarch has a black head and breast, white belly, and white wing patches, found in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland and hill forests, often following mixed-species foraging flocks. Its song is a series of whistled notes.

children.name: Symposiachrus bimaculatus
children.description: The Moluccan Monarch has a black head, white throat, and chestnut underparts with two distinctive white patches on the wings, found in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland and montane forests. Its name refers to the twin white wing patches.

children.name: Symposiachrus mundus
children.description: The Black-bibbed Monarch has a black head and bib, white underparts, and chestnut flanks, endemic to the islands of Tanimbar and Babar in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the mid-storey.

children.name: Symposiachrus manadensis
children.description: The Hooded Monarch has a black head and hood, white throat, and chestnut underparts, found in the lowland forests of the Moluccas and coastal New Guinea. It forages actively in the canopy and is often seen in pairs. Its call is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Symposiachrus barbatus
children.description: The Black-tailed Monarch has a black head, white throat, chestnut underparts, and a black tail, found in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy for insects. Its song is a series of whistled notes.

children.name: Symposiachrus infelix
children.description: The Manus Monarch is a dark-plumaged monarch with a black head, brownish upperparts, and rufous underparts, endemic to Manus Island in the Admiralty Islands. It inhabits lowland forests and is poorly known. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Symposiachrus verticalis
children.description: The Black-tailed Monarch has a black head, white throat, and chestnut underparts with a black tail, found in the Bismarck Archipelago. It inhabits lowland forests.

children.name: Carterornis
children.description: A genus of strikingly coloured monarchs with white, yellow, or golden plumage and black masks or markings, found in Australia and the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Carterornis chrysomela
children.description: The Golden Monarch is a brilliant golden-yellow bird with a black mask and throat, found in lowland rainforests of New Guinea and the surrounding islands. It is an active forager that gleans insects from foliage in the canopy.

children.name: Carterornis leucotis
children.description: The White-eared Monarch has a blue-grey head, white ear patch, white underparts, and rich chestnut wings. It is a shy inhabitant of lowland rainforests and gallery forests of north-eastern Australia.

children.name: Carterornis pileatus
children.description: The White-naped Monarch has a black head with a white nape patch, white underparts, and golden-yellow wings and back, found in the Moluccas. It inhabits lowland forests and forages in the canopy for insects. Its bright plumage makes it one of the most colourful monarchs.

children.name: Arses
children.description: A genus of small, strikingly patterned monarchs with frilled or ruffled neck feathers and pale blue eye-rings, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and surrounding islands.

children.name: Arses insularis
children.description: The Ochre-collared Monarch has a black head, white throat and breast, and a distinctive ochre-orange collar across the nape. It inhabits lowland rainforests of New Guinea.

children.name: Arses telescopthalmus
children.description: The Frilled Monarch has a black head and breast, prominent white frilled neck feathers, and a prominent pale blue eye-ring. It is a lively forager of lowland and hill forests in New Guinea.

children.name: Arses lorealis
children.description: The northern form of the Frilled Monarch, found in the lowland forests of northern New Guinea. It has a black head, white frilled neck feathers, and a blue eye-ring. It forages actively in the mid-storey.

children.name: Clytorhynchus
children.description: A genus of monarchs known as shrikebills, named for their stout, hooked bills adapted for tearing bark and searching for insects. They are found on islands of the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Clytorhynchus pachycephaloides
children.description: The Southern Shrikebill has olive-brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a distinctive thick, hooked bill used to pry bark from branches in search of prey. It is found in forests of New Caledonia and Vanuatu.

children.name: Clytorhynchus vitiensis
children.description: The Fiji Shrikebill has olive-brown upperparts, greyish underparts, and a stout hooked bill, found across the islands of Fiji. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, using its strong bill to pry bark from branches in search of insects. It is often seen in mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Clytorhynchus nigrogularis
children.description: The Black-throated Shrikebill has a distinctive black throat contrasting with olive-brown upperparts and a pale belly, found in the forests of Fiji. It uses its thick, hooked bill to tear bark and probe for insects. It is classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Clytorhynchus hamlini
children.description: The Rennell Shrikebill is endemic to Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands, with olive-brown upperparts and pale underparts. It inhabits the island's unique raised coral limestone forests. Its strong, hooked bill enables it to forage for insects under bark.

children.name: Clytorhynchus sanctaecrucis
children.description: The Santa Cruz Shrikebill is endemic to the Santa Cruz Islands in the Solomon Islands, with olive-brown upperparts, a pale throat, and a stout hooked bill. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects by probing bark and dead wood.

children.name: Metabolus
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch restricted to the island of Chuuk in Micronesia. The single species is a striking bird with deep blue-black plumage and a distinctive white tail.

children.name: Metabolus rugensis
children.description: The Chuuk Monarch is a striking bird with deep blue-black plumage, a white tail, and a pale blue-grey bill, endemic to the island of Chuuk in Micronesia. It inhabits lowland forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range and habitat loss.

children.name: Eutrichomyias
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch flycatcher found only on the island of Sangir in Indonesia. The single species is a stunning cerulean-blue bird with a long, forked tail and was once thought to be extinct.

children.name: Eutrichomyias rowleyi
children.description: The Cerulean Flycatcher is a brilliant cerulean-blue monarch with a long, deeply forked tail, endemic to the island of Sangir in Indonesia. It was once thought to be extinct until its rediscovery in the late 1990s. It inhabits montane forests and is classified as Critically Endangered due to deforestation.

children.name: Neolalage
children.description: A monotypic genus of monarch flycatcher found in Vanuatu. The single species has a distinctive pale yellow belly and is often encountered in pairs or small family groups.

children.name: Neolalage banksiana
children.description: The Buff-bellied Monarch has olive-green upperparts, a grey head, and a pale buff-yellow belly, found in the rainforests of Vanuatu. It forages actively in the canopy for insects, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its call is a series of thin, high-pitched notes.

children.name: Chasiempis
children.description: A genus of three closely related monarch flycatchers endemic to the Hawaiian Islands, where they are known as elepaios. They are the only monarchs native to Hawaii and show remarkable variation between the islands in plumage and behaviour.

children.name: Chasiempis sandwichensis
children.description: The Hawaii Elepaio is endemic to the Big Island of Hawaii, with brown upperparts, white underparts, and a characteristic upright posture. It is an active, acrobatic forager that fans its tail constantly. It is found in native forests from sea level to the tree line and is known for its complex, whistled song.

children.name: Chasiempis sclateri
children.description: The Kauai Elepaio has brown upperparts, white underparts with a buffy wash, and a long, white-tipped tail, endemic to the island of Kauai in Hawaii. It is a bold, curious bird that will approach humans closely, but has declined due to avian malaria. It is classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Chasiempis ibidis
children.description: The Oahu Elepaio is the most endangered of the three elepaio species, endemic to the island of Oahu. It has brown upperparts and white underparts with extensive white in the tail. It has declined dramatically due to avian malaria and habitat loss. It is classified as Endangered.

children.name: Pomarea
children.description: A genus of monarchs restricted to the islands of the south Pacific, with many species now critically endangered or extinct. They show a range of plumage colours from black to chestnut and white, and are named for the Pomarea Islands of French Polynesia.

children.name: Pomarea nigra
children.description: The Tahiti Monarch is a glossy black monarch with a slightly forked tail, endemic to the island of Tahiti. It inhabits native forests in the valleys of Tahiti, foraging for insects in the mid-storey. It is classified as Critically Endangered with fewer than 100 individuals remaining.

children.name: Pomarea mendozae
children.description: The Marquesan Monarch has dark brown upperparts and pale underparts, endemic to the Marquesas Islands of French Polynesia. It inhabits native forests on the islands of Hiva Oa and Tahuata. It is classified as Critically Endangered due to habitat loss and invasive predators.

children.name: Pomarea iphis
children.description: The Iphis Monarch is a dark brown monarch with pale underparts, endemic to the island of Ua Huka in the Marquesas Islands. It inhabits native dry forests and forages for insects in the canopy. It is classified as Critically Endangered with a very small population.

children.name: Pomarea whitneyi
children.description: The Fatuhiva Monarch is a chestnut-brown monarch with a long tail, endemic to the island of Fatu Hiva in the Marquesas Islands. It inhabits native forests in the interior of the island. It is classified as Critically Endangered with fewer than 50 individuals remaining.

children.name: Pomarea mira
children.description: The Ua Pou Monarch was endemic to the island of Ua Pou in the Marquesas Islands, with dark brown upperparts and pale underparts. It has not been reliably recorded since the 1980s and is likely extinct. It was last seen in native forests on the island's interior ridges.

children.name: Pomarea nukuhivae
children.description: The Nuku Hiva Monarch was endemic to the island of Nuku Hiva in the Marquesas Islands, with a black head and breast, chestnut back, and white belly. It has not been reliably recorded since the late 1930s and is considered extinct, a victim of habitat loss and introduced predators.

children.name: Pomarea fluxa
children.description: The Eiao Monarch was endemic to the island of Eiao in the Marquesas Islands. It is known from only a few specimens and has not been recorded with certainty since the 1970s. It is likely extinct, driven by habitat degradation from feral sheep.

children.name: Pomarea dimidiata
children.description: The Rarotonga Monarch is a dark brown bird with a pale belly, endemic to the island of Rarotonga in the Cook Islands. It was once reduced to just 30 birds but has recovered through intensive conservation efforts to several hundred individuals. It is classified as Endangered.

children.name: Pomarea pomarea
children.description: The Tubuai Monarch was endemic to the island of Tubuai in the Austral Islands of French Polynesia. It is known only from subfossil remains and may have gone extinct shortly after human settlement of the island. It represents the southernmost known range of the Pomarea genus.

children.name: Mayrornis
children.description: A genus of small monarchs restricted to the islands of Fiji and Tonga in the southwest Pacific. They have relatively plain plumage of grey, brown, and white, and are active, acrobatic foragers that frequently fan their tails.

children.name: Mayrornis schistaceus
children.description: The Slaty Monarch has blue-grey upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a long, white-tipped tail that it constantly fans. It is endemic to the island of Vanua Levu and nearby islands in Fiji, inhabiting lowland and montane forests. It is an active forager that gleans insects from foliage in the mid-storey.

children.name: Mayrornis lessoni
children.description: The Slaty Flycatcher has grey upperparts, whitish underparts, and a long, graduated tail, found in the forests of Fiji. It is an active, acrobatic forager that fans its tail frequently while gleaning insects from foliage. Its song is a series of thin, high-pitched notes.

children.name: Mayrornis versicolor
children.description: The Ogea Monarch has olive-brown upperparts and pale yellowish underparts, endemic to the islands of Ogea in the Lau Archipelago of Fiji. It inhabits native forests and forages for insects in the understorey. It is classified as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Trochocercus
children.description: A genus of small crested monarchs found in the forests of sub-Saharan Africa. They are named for their distinctive erectile crests and are among the most colourful of African monarchs, with males showing glossy blue-black or slate-grey plumage.

children.name: Trochocercus cyanomelas
children.description: A small, crested monarch with the male a glossy blue-black above and white below, and the female olive-grey, found across eastern and southern Africa. It is an active forager of forest canopies, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its prominent erectile crest and constant tail-flicking make it easily identifiable.

children.name: Trochocercus nitens
children.description: A small, crested monarch with the male a dark slate-grey above and pale grey below, found in the lowland rainforests of West and Central Africa. It is an active, acrobatic forager that fans its tail and erects its crest frequently. It often joins mixed-species foraging flocks in the forest canopy.
